'''Daryl Anthony Powell''' (born 21 July 1965) is an English professional [[rugby league]] coach who is the head coach of [[Wakefield Trinity]] in the [[Super League]] and a former professional rugby league footballer.

He played as a {{rlp|so}} in the 1980s, 1990s and 2000s for the [[Sheffield Eagles]], the [[Keighley Cougars]] and the [[Leeds Rhinos]], as well as [[Balmain Tigers]] and [[Gold Coast Seagulls]] in Australia. He played more than 450 games including 33 caps for [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]], and made appearances in the [[Super League]] Grand Final and in two [[Challenge Cup]] Finals for Leeds.<ref name="Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/><ref name="Coach Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/> At Sheffield he formed a key partnership with [[Mark Aston]], and holds the club record for the most individual tries in a match (5) and he did hold the record for most career tries (114) until this was broken by [[Menzie Yere]] in 2013.

Powell's first move into coaching was with [[Keighley Cougars]] as a player-coach. After retiring, he became head coach of the [[Leeds Rhinos]] in 2001, and later became their director of rugby. He coached the [[Ireland national rugby league team]] as well as [[rugby union]] side [[Leeds Tykes]], before a return to domestic rugby league in 2008 with [[Featherstone Rovers]] in the [[RFL Championship|Championship]] who he led to three consecutive first-placed finishes. In 2013, he was appointed head coach of the [[Castleford Tigers]] in the [[Super League]] where, across nine seasons, he won the [[League Leaders' Shield]] and reached three major finals. He coached the [[Warrington Wolves]] for two seasons before taking charge of [[Wakefield Trinity]] ahead of the 2024 campaign.

==Background==
Powell was born in [[Ackworth, West Yorkshire|Ackworth]], [[Wakefield]], [[West Riding of Yorkshire]], England.

==Playing career==
Powell was selected to go on the [[1992 Great Britain Lions tour of Australia and New Zealand]]. His [[Testimonial match]] at Sheffield Eagles took place in 1994. He played for England in the [[1995 Rugby League World Cup]].{{citation needed|date=October 2022}}

===Leeds Rhinos===
Powell played for [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]] at stand-off half in their [[1998 Super League Grand Final]] loss to [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]].<ref name="Super League III 1998 - Results">{{cite web |title=Super League III 1998 - Results |url=http://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/seasons/super-league-iii-1998/results.html |website=Rugby League Project |access-date=27 May 2020}}</ref> He was a member of the Rhinos' [[1999 Challenge Cup]] winning team. He played stand-off in the final as the Rhinos beat [[London Broncos]] 52–16 to claim their first silverware since 1978.<ref>{{cite web|title=Sport: Rugby League Rhino charge overpowers Broncos|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/sport/rugby_league/333483.stm|work=BBC Sport|access-date=26 January 2014|date=1 May 1999}}</ref>

==Coaching career==
===Keighley Cougars===
Powell's first coaching appointment was as player/coach at Keighley where he was appointed at the end of the 1996 season. He was in charge for 24 games before his transfer to Leeds at the end of June 1997 although his tenure as coach at Keighley continued until July 1997. Under his leadership Keighley won 14, lost 9 and drew 1 game.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Fletcher |first1=Raymond |last2=Howes |first2= David |name-list-style=amp |title=Rothman's Rugby League Yearbook 1998 |date=1998 |isbn=978-0747-276-838 |page=80|publisher=Headline Publishing }}</ref>

===Leeds Rhinos===
After Powell retired from playing in 2001, he became the head coach of [[Leeds Rhinos]]. The Rhinos appeared to be on an upward trajectory under Powell, finishing [[Super League VI|5th]] then [[Super League VII|4th]] in his first two seasons.{{citation needed|date=October 2022}}

In his third season in charge, the club reached the [[2003 Challenge Cup|2003 Challenge Cup Final]] but lost 22–20 to [[Bradford Bulls]]. Trailing by 2 points with 5 minutes remaining, the Rhinos were awarded a penalty. Captain [[Kevin Sinfield]] famously opted not to take a shot at goal which would have levelled the scores. The decision backfired with the Bulls' defensive line holding firm to deny the Rhinos their first major silverware in over 30 years. The cup final defeat did not appear to affect Leeds' [[Super League VIII|Super League]] form as they established a commanding lead at the top of the table. In spite of this, it was announced in July 2003 that Powell would be moved into a director of rugby role the following season to make way for [[Tony Smith (Australian rugby league coach)|Tony Smith]] to take over as head coach.<ref name="Powell handed a time-out by Leeds">{{cite web |title=Powell handed a time-out by Leeds |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2003/jul/31/rugbyleague.leedsrhinos |website=The Guardian |date=31 July 2003|access-date=26 May 2020}}</ref> Following the announcement, the Rhinos form dipped and they surrendered their lead at the top of the table to the [[Bradford Bulls]]. They then crashed out of the play-offs without winning a game.<ref name="Super League VIII Results">{{cite web |title=Super League VIII Results |url=http://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/seasons/super-league-viii-2003/results.html |website=Rugby League Project |access-date=26 May 2020}}</ref>

During his tenure as Rhinos coach, he handed debuts to numerous future club legends including [[Danny McGuire]], [[Rob Burrow]], [[Matt Diskin]] and [[Ryan Bailey (rugby league)|Ryan Bailey]]. He also handed, a then 22-year old, [[Kevin Sinfield]] the captaincy.<ref name="Daryl Powell's road to the Grand Final as Castleford face Leeds">{{cite web |title=Daryl Powell's road to the Grand Final as Castleford face Leeds |url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12197/11067647/daryl-powells-road-to-the-grand-final-as-castleford-face-leeds |website=Sky Sports |access-date=27 May 2020}}</ref> The group would go on to form the nucleus of the clubs famous "Golden generation".<ref name="As Good As It Gets: The story of Leeds Rhinos' golden generation">{{cite web |title=As Good As It Gets: The story of Leeds Rhinos' golden generation |url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/as-good-as-it-gets-the-story-of-leeds-rhinos-golden-generation/ |website=Love Rugby League |date=19 March 2018 |access-date=27 May 2020}}</ref>

===Ireland===
In 2004 Powell was coach for the [[Ireland national rugby league team|Ireland]] national team.<ref>{{cite news |title=Ireland plan to give it a go |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/international_and_australian/3986847.stm |work=BBC Sport |date=5 November 2004}}</ref> He helped to lead the team to the final of the [[2004 European Nations Cup]] where they went down 36–12 to [[England national rugby league team|England]].<ref name="Powell proud of plucky Irish">{{cite web |title=Powell proud of plucky Irish |url=https://www.therhinos.co.uk/2004/11/09/powell-proud-of-plucky-irish/ |website=Leeds Rhinos |date=9 November 2004|access-date=27 May 2020}}</ref>

===Leeds Tykes===
In July 2005 he made the switch to [[rugby union]] when he became the offensive coach and matchday manager for [[Leeds Tykes]]. In January 2006 he was promoted to head coach, but the season ended in disappointment when the Tykes were [[promotion and relegation|relegated]].{{citation needed|date=October 2022}}

===Featherstone Rovers===
He was appointed head coach of [[Featherstone Rovers]] in September 2008.<ref name="Powell named Featherstone coach">{{cite web |title=Powell named Featherstone coach |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/7604507.stm |website=BBC Sport |date=8 September 2008|access-date=26 May 2020}}</ref>

He oversaw one of the most successful periods in Featherstone Rovers' history, leading them to three consecutive [[RFL Championship|Championship]] League Leaders' Shields and Grand Finals between 2010 and 2012.<ref name="Daryl Powell: Castleford Tigers appoint Featherstone coach">{{cite web |title=Daryl Powell: Castleford Tigers appoint Featherstone coach |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/22432467 |website=BBC Sport |date=7 May 2013|access-date=26 May 2020}}</ref> Despite winning the [[2011 RFL Championship|Championship Grand Final]] in 2011, Featherstone were not promoted to [[super league]] due to the controversial [[Super League licensing|licensing system]] in place at the time.<ref name="Elston welcomes wake-up call">{{cite web |title=Elston welcomes wake-up call |url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12208/7062297/elston-welcomes-wake-up-call |date=26 July 2011|access-date=26 May 2020}}</ref> Subsequently, Powell continued to ply his trade in the second tier in 2012. In addition to again, leading the club to the [[2012 RFL Championship|2012]] league leaders shield and grand final, Powell also masterminded a famous victory over Super League side and local rivals [[Castleford Tigers|Castleford]] in the Challenge Cup in what would prove to be his final season at the club.<ref name="Featherstone 23 - 16 Castleford">{{cite web |title=Featherstone 23 - 16 Castleford |url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/featherstone-vs-castleford/35628 |website=Sky Sports | date=14 April 2012|access-date=26 May 2020}}</ref>

===Castleford Tigers===
On 7 May 2013, Powell was announced as the new head coach of struggling [[Super League]] side [[Castleford Tigers]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=7 May 2013 |title=Castleford present Daryl Powell to media as the Tigers' new head coach |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2013/may/07/castleford-daryl-powell-head-coach |access-date=4 February 2024 |website=The Guardian}}</ref><ref name="Daryl Powell joins Castleford">{{cite web |title=Daryl Powell joins Castleford |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/general/rugby-league/daryl-powell-joins-castleford-8606850.html |website=The Independent|date=7 May 2013 |access-date=26 May 2020}}</ref>

Powell was awarded the Coach of the Year award for 2014, after getting the club to their first Wembley appearance for twenty years and their highest league position since Super League began. He coached the club to the [[2014 Challenge Cup Final]] defeat by the [[Leeds Rhinos]] at [[Wembley Stadium]].<ref name="Leeds lift Challenge Cup after Ryan Hall’s double stuns Castleford">{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2014/aug/23/catleford-leeds-challenge-cup-final-match-report|title=Leeds lift Challenge Cup after Ryan Hall's double stuns Castleford|work=The Guardian|date=23 August 2014|access-date=13 November 2019}}</ref>

Powell oversaw arguably one of Castleford's greatest ever seasons in [[2017 Castleford Tigers season|2017]] when they won the League Leaders' Shield by topping the table for the first time in their history.<ref name="Castleford win league leaders shield for first time">{{cite web |title=Castleford win league leaders shield for first time |url=http://www.sport.bt.com/rugby-league/castleford-win-league-leaders-shield-for-first-time-S11364204765897 |website=BT Sport |date= 17 August 2017|access-date=26 May 2020}}</ref> Despite finishing first in the regular season by a record margin, a first top flight title continued to allude Powell as the Tigers were defeated in the [[2017 Super League Grand Final]] by the [[Leeds Rhinos]] at [[Old Trafford]].<ref name="Castleford 6-24 Leeds: Grand Final 2017 – as it happened">{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/live/2017/oct/07/castleford-v-leeds-grand-final-2017-live-super-league-rugby?page=with:block-59d9128de4b00593b4d67a05#liveblog-navigation|title=Castleford 6-24 Leeds: Grand Final 2017 – as it happened|work=The Guardian|date=7 October 2017|access-date=6 November 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.com/sport/rugby-league/41536394|title=Grand Final 2017: Castleford 6-24 Leeds Rhinos|date=7 October 2017|work=BBC Sport|access-date=6 November 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2017/oct/07/castleford-leeds-super-league-grand-final-match-report|title=Danny McGuire guides Leeds to Grand Final success over Castleford|date=7 October 2017|work=The Guardian|access-date=6 November 2019}}</ref>

===Warrington Wolves===
On 19 March 2021, before the start of the delayed 2021 season, it was announced that Powell would leave Castleford Tigers at the end of the campaign, with early rumours linking him to Warrington Wolves, whose Head Coach [[Steve Price (rugby league, born 1977)|Steve Price]] had also announced he would be leaving at the end of the 2021 campaign. Daryl Powell was announced as Warrington Wolves coach on 6 April 2021, taking over at the start of the 2022 season.

On 30 July 2023, an official statement from [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]] confirmed, that Powell (alongside assistant coach [[Ryan Sheridan (rugby league)|Ryan Sheridan]]), had left the club by mutual consent, after losing a 6th consecutive game to bottom-placed [[Wakefield Trinity]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/warrington-wolves-confirm-immediate-departure-of-head-coach-daryl-powell|title=Warrington Wolves confirm immediate departure of Daryl Powell.|date=30 July 2023|work=loverugbyleague}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/66301927|title=Super League: Wakefield Trinity 42-6 Warrington Wolves - Innes Senior claims four tries in victory|work=BBC Sport }}</ref>

===Wakefield Trinity===
On 24 September 2023, it was announced that Powell would take over as head coach on a four-year deal following Wakefield's relegation from Super League.<ref>{{Cite web |date=24 Sep 2023 |title=Daryl Powell agrees huge deal with Wakefield Trinity |url=https://www.seriousaboutrl.com/daryl-powell-agrees-huge-deal-with-wakefield-trinity-82484/ |website=Serious About RL}}</ref>

On 8 June 2024, Powell led the club to victory in the [[RFL 1895 Cup]] at Wembley Stadium defeating Sheffield Eagles 50-6.<ref>{{Cite web |date=8 Jun 2024 |title=Sheffield Eagles 6-50 Wakefield Trinity |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/match/EVP4478124 |website=BBC Sport}}</ref>
Wakefield would then go on to claim the league leaders shield having lost only one match throughout the regular season. The club would reach the 2024 RFL Championship grand final where they defeated Toulouse Olympique 36-0.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/articles/cqjr5lvxk4lo|title=Jowitt breaks points record as Wakefield cruise to Grand Final win|website=www.bbc.co.uk}}</ref>
In the 2025 Super League season, Powell guided Wakefield to a 6th placed finish on the table which saw the club qualify for the playoffs for the first time in 13 years. Wakefield would be defeated in the elimination playoff by Leigh.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.seriousaboutrl.com/rugby-league-news-what-next-for-wakefield-trinity-after-incredible-super-league-return-ends-in-play-off-loss-108937/|title=What next for Wakefield Trinity after incredible Super League return ends in play-off loss|website=www.seriousaboutrl.com}}</ref>
